nvm on Windows: Fixing "Access is denied" and "exit status 145"

As a Windows dev, you'll probably hit "nvm use exit status 1: Access is denied" or "exit status 145: The directory is not empty." at some point with nvm-windows. Both are common — writing down how I solved them so future-me has notes.
nvm use exit status 1: Access is denied
This one's a permissions issue. You'll hit it on basically any nvm command:
language-cmd shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast

nvm -v
nvm list
nvm use xxx
...
The fix is simple — right-click your terminal (PowerShell, cmd, Windows Terminal, whatever) and pick "Run as administrator". nvm starts working immediately.
Right-clicking every time gets old. You can set your terminal to always run as admin. Right-click the app → Properties:
OK → Apply. Now it opens elevated every time. For reference, here's what admin vs. normal looks like:
exit status 145: The directory is not empty
This one's trickier. Most search hits blame spaces or non-English characters in the install path — default is
. Easiest "fix" they recommend is reinstalling with a custom path. I'm skeptical though, because my install is at the default
So I went looking elsewhere. First check is to make sure nvm's
language-js shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
Then I found this comment on
exit status 145: The directory is not empty , when I run command : nvm use 16.10.0

For me the issue was "C:\Program Files (x86)\nodejs" folder was indeed non-empty after uninstalling nodejs.
Deleting that folder than "nvm use .." worked.
This is actually a Windows quirk — Windows doesn't always fully clean out folders when you uninstall something. The leftover folder causes the
too) and the error goes away.
. Heads up — it's a symlink, not a real folder. If you see something like this icon, you're good:
You can also see the symlink points back into the nvm install:
If you see this instead (real folder, not a symlink), delete it — wrong state:
This wrong state also causes a weird symptom —

node : The term 'node' is not recognized as the name of a cmdlet, function, script file, or operable program. Check the spelling of the name, or if a path was included, verify that the path is correct and try again.
So that's another way to detect it. Don't try to fix this by creating your own shortcut — there are parameter settings that won't be set right. Instead, run
from an elevated terminal — nvm will create the proper symlink at

Two common nvm errors on Windows — "nvm use exit status 1: Access is denied" and "exit status 145: The directory is not empty." Quick notes on both — the first is almost always a permissions issue (run as administrator), and the second is usually a leftover nodejs folder from a previous install that nvm can't overwrite.
